mechanism: Inhibits mitochondrial complex I and oxidative phosphorylation, reducing ATP production and cancer cell proliferation
additional_notes: Primary use: type 2 diabetes; AML cells (including FLT3‑mut) show metabolic vulnerability to OXPHOS inhibition; growth‑inhibitory effects often appear ≥0.5–1 mM

mechanism: Disrupts mitochondrial function and inhibits signaling pathways including Wnt/β‑catenin and STAT3
additional_notes: Reported anti‑leukemic activity; leukemia IC50 values often in low micromolar range

mechanism: Inhibits aldehyde dehydrogenase and induces proteasome inhibition/ROS stress, targeting ALDH‑high leukemia stem/progenitor cells
additional_notes: Activity enhanced by addition of 1–2 µM CuSO4 (copper); many blood cancer cell lines show IC50 ≈ 50–500 nM with copper

category: Antiepileptic; mood stabilizer; weak HDAC inhibitor
mechanism: Inhibits histone deacetylases leading to epigenetic modulation and differentiation in AML cells
additional_notes: Also known as valproate; often combined with ATRA or hypomethylating agents; AML effects typically in 0.3–2 mM range

mechanism: Inhibits class I and II histone deacetylases, causing acetylation of histones and non‑histone proteins and inducing apoptosis
additional_notes: Also known as SAHA; approved for CTCL; AML IC50 often 0.2–2 µM; MOLM‑13 commonly responds in sub‑ to low‑micromolar range

mechanism: Broad inhibition of histone deacetylases, leading to chromatin remodeling, apoptosis, and synergy with FLT3 inhibitors
additional_notes: Approved for multiple myeloma; AML IC50 values ~5–50 nM; expected potent activity in FLT3‑ITD AML such as MOLM‑13

mechanism: Binds cereblon E3 ligase complex leading to degradation of transcription factors and immunomodulatory/anti‑proliferative effects
additional_notes: Representative IMiD (with thalidomide); primary use in multiple myeloma and MDS with del(5q); direct AML cytotoxic IC50 often >1–5 µM; may show differentiation or combinatorial effects

mechanism: Binds tubulin and disrupts microtubule formation, leading to mitotic arrest and apoptosis
additional_notes: Reported anti‑leukemia activity; AML cell line IC50s frequently 0.1–5 µM

mechanism: Primarily inhibits serotonin reuptake; in leukemia cells can induce mitochondrial and ER stress leading to cytotoxicity
additional_notes: Representative SSRI; leukemia IC50 values commonly ~5–30 µM; related SSRIs (fluoxetine, sertraline) have similar cytotoxic profiles

mechanism: Inhibits serotonin reuptake and can directly induce apoptosis in AML cells, with reported synergy with BCL‑2 inhibition
additional_notes: AML IC50 usually ~5–20 µM; studied in combination with BCL‑2 inhibitors

mechanism: Generates reactive oxygen species and disrupts mitochondrial function via redox cycling, inducing apoptosis
additional_notes: Primary use in leprosy; published anti‑AML activity including FLT3‑ITD AML; AML IC50 typically 0.1–3 µM

mechanism: Inhibits bacterial and mitochondrial protein synthesis, leading to mitochondrial stress and impaired leukemia cell growth
additional_notes: Effects on AML cell proliferation often observed in the 10–100 µM range

mechanism: Inhibits fungal lanosterol 14α‑demethylase and in cancer cells interferes with Hedgehog signaling, angiogenesis, and cholesterol trafficking
additional_notes: Some preclinical leukemia data; many cancer cell IC50 values 0.5–5 µM

mechanism: Blocks histamine H1 receptor and hERG/K+ channels, with off‑target effects that can induce apoptosis in leukemia cells
additional_notes: Withdrawn clinically for QT prolongation/torsades but useful in vitro; leukemia IC50 often ~1–10 µM; astemizole is a related alternative

mechanism: Inhibits HMG‑CoA reductase, blocking mevalonate pathway and depleting prenylation substrates required for survival signaling
additional_notes: Representative statin; AML cell lines can be sensitive to statin‑induced apoptosis; AML IC50 commonly 1–10 µM

mechanism: Inhibits HMG‑CoA reductase and mevalonate pathway, reducing prenylation of signaling proteins and inducing apoptosis
additional_notes: Primary use: hyperlipidemia; some studies suggest potent anti‑leukemic effects at micromolar concentrations; recommended to test overlapping doses with simvastatin

mechanism: Raises lysosomal pH and inhibits autophagosome‑lysosome fusion, blocking autophagic flux and sensitizing AML cells to cytotoxic stress
additional_notes: Considered along with chloroquine; direct AML IC50 often ~20–60 µM; also used in autoimmune diseases

mechanism: Blocks β1 and β2 adrenergic receptors, exerting anti‑angiogenic and direct anti‑tumor/anti‑proliferative effects
additional_notes: Some data in hematologic malignancies and leukemia models; leukemic cells often respond in 5–50 µM range

mechanism: Inhibits BCR‑ABL, KIT, and PDGFR tyrosine kinases, blocking downstream proliferation/survival signaling
additional_notes: Primary use in CML, Ph+ ALL, and GIST; not effective for FLT3‑ITD but useful as benchmark/control; AML cells without BCR‑ABL often resistant (>5–10 µM)

category: Second‑generation ABL/Src family tyrosine kinase inhibitor
mechanism: Potently inhibits BCR‑ABL and multiple other kinases (including some Src family and KIT), suppressing oncogenic signaling
additional_notes: Representative second‑generation ABL/Src inhibitor (with dasatinib); primary use in CML; sensitive cells often have IC50 10–200 nM; MOLM‑13 may be less sensitive but useful as kinase‑inhibitor control
